messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":43,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"electrician","city_of_living":"Chicago","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"upper secondary","parent_2":"primary school"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["travel","knitting","yoga"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":97,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":40,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":10,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":6,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":27,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a person who is naturally anxious but also highly open to new experiences, I've had to navigate some tricky conversations around COVID vaccines. My anxiety makes me hesitant in group settings where people are passionately debating these topics online—especially since my husband works in customer service at an insurance company and they often talk about it during family dinners. However, because of my high openness score (97), I'm also curious enough to understand why others might be resistant despite the overwhelming scientific consensus.

I started by educating myself thoroughly using reliable sources like CDC guidelines and peer-reviewed journals accessible through public libraries since I don't have advanced degrees or medical training beyond vocational work as an electrician. This gave me confidence during discussions at home with my two kids who ask lots of questions about school being in person versus remote these days.

My husband's profession actually helps; he can explain the economic impact side more effectively than someone without his background might do, while I bring up ethical considerations from reading Hindu texts where compassion towards all beings is emphasized. We make sure to cite specific numbers like vaccination efficacy rates or current infection statistics whenever possible rather than just saying 'experts say'.

The low extraversion and agreeableness scores mean that being in these discussions feels draining sometimes, so I usually limit myself to one conversation per week via Zoom calls with friends who work as healthcare professionals. But honestly? The anxiety level drops when focusing on facts instead of emotions—especially knowing we're protecting ourselves against variants while traveling this summer.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1) Used self-directed education through reliable sources given my high openness and moderate conscientiousness to build confidence in discussions.

2) Leveraged husband's professional background (customer service/insurance industry) as complementary expertise for our family conversations about healthcare topics.

3) Incorporated ethical considerations from religious studies knowledge while maintaining factual focus due to low neuroticism anxiety levels.",
  "tone": "Calm yet concerned, balanced with curiosity and compassion",
  "citations": ["Centers for Disease Control and Prevention", "World Health Organization"],
  "used_persona_fields": ["psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person who is naturally anxious but also highly open to new experiences, I've had to navigate some tricky conversations around COVID vaccines. My anxiety makes me hesitant in group settings where people are passionately debating these topics online—especially since my husband works in customer service at an insurance company and they often talk about it during family dinners. However, because of my high openness score (97), I'm also curious enough to understand why others might be resistant despite the overwhelming scientific consensus.

I started by educating myself thoroughly using reliable sources like CDC guidelines and peer-reviewed journals accessible through public libraries since I don't have advanced degrees or medical training beyond vocational work as an electrician. This gave me confidence during discussions at home with my two kids who ask lots of questions about school being in person versus remote these days.

My husband's profession actually helps; he can explain the economic impact side more effectively than someone without his background might do, while I bring up ethical considerations from reading Hindu texts where compassion towards all beings is emphasized. We make sure to cite specific numbers like vaccination efficacy rates or current infection statistics whenever possible rather than just saying 'experts say'.

The low extraversion and agreeableness scores mean that being in these discussions feels draining sometimes, so I usually limit myself to one conversation per week via Zoom calls with friends who work as healthcare professionals. But honestly? The anxiety level drops when focusing on facts instead of emotions—especially knowing we're protecting ourselves against variants while traveling this summer.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1) Used self-directed education through reliable sources given my high openness and moderate conscientiousness to build confidence in discussions.

2) Leveraged husband's professional background (customer service/insurance industry) as complementary expertise for our family conversations about healthcare topics.

3) Incorporated ethical considerations from religious studies knowledge while maintaining factual focus due to low neuroticism anxiety levels.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet concerned, balanced with curiosity and compassion
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
